questions.hint: A nonzero discriminant means no cusp or node.
questions.explain: A nonzero discriminant means no cusp or node.

questions.q: A singular cubic typically has what local defect?
questions.hint: Singularity appears as node/cusp where derivatives vanish simultaneously.
questions.explain: Singularity appears as node/cusp where derivatives vanish simultaneously.

hard.q: The singular Weierstrass cubic $y^2=x^3$ (a cusp at the origin) has smooth locus isomorphic (as a group) to:
hard.explain: A cuspidal cubic has smooth locus $\cong\mathbb{G}_a$ (the additive group), parametrised by $t\mapsto(t^2,t^3)$. A nodal cubic has smooth locus $\cong\mathbb{G}_m$.

hard.q: For $E:y^2=x^3+x^2$ (a node at the origin, $a=0,b=0$ in Weierstrass form after the change $x\mapsto x-1/3$ is NOT yet done), compute $4a^3+27b^2$ directly from $a=0,b=0$.
Which is the correct order of steps to verify $y^2=x^3-3x+2$ is singular?
choices: Check $\Delta\ne 0$, then find the node, then confirm genus $1$
Compute $f=x^3-3x+2$, find a double root by solving $f=f'=0$ simultaneously, then confirm $\Delta=0$
Compute the $j$-invariant; if $j=0$ the curve is singular
Verify the point $(1,0)$ is rational; all rational points imply singularity
hard.explain: $f(1)=1-3+2=0$ and $f'(1)=3-3=0$, so $x=1$ is a double root, giving a node at $(1,0)$. One then confirms $\Delta=-16(4(-3)^3+27(2)^2)=-16(-108+108)=0$.

questions.q: On an elliptic curve, $P+Q+R=\mathcal O$ when:
questions.hint: The secant line meets the cubic in three points summing to zero.
questions.explain: The secant line meets the cubic in three points summing to zero.

questions.q: The identity element for the elliptic-curve group law is:
questions.hint: Projectively, the neutral element is the point at infinity.
questions.explain: Projectively, the neutral element is the point at infinity.

questions.q: If a point has order $5$, the smallest positive $n$ with $nP=\mathcal O$ is:
questions.hint: That is the definition of order.
questions.explain: That is the definition of order.

hard.q: The slick proof that the chord-and-tangent group law is associative uses:
choices: Explicit verification of all $27$ cases by coordinate computation
The Riemann–Roch isomorphism $E\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Pic}^0(E)$, $P\mapsto[P]-[O]$, transporting the group structure from $\operatorname{Pic}^0(E)$
The fact that every cubic curve is a group scheme
hard.hint: Transport the group structure from a place where associativity is obvious.
hard.explain: One identifies $E$ with $\operatorname{Pic}^0(E)$ via $P\mapsto[P]-[O]$; since $\operatorname{Pic}^0$ is a group by construction, associativity is automatic and one avoids all coordinate computation.

hard.q: On $E: y^2=x^3-x$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, the $2$-torsion points satisfy $y=0$. How many affine $2$-torsion points does $E$ have?
hard.explain: $y=0$ gives $x^3-x=x(x-1)(x+1)=0$, so $x\in\{0,1,-1\}$: three affine points $(0,0),(1,0),(-1,0)$. Together with $\mathcal O$, the full $E[2]\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ has $4$ elements.

hard.q: The degree of the multiplication-by-$n$ map $[n]:E\to E$ is:
hard.explain: $\deg[n]=n^2$, so the kernel $E[n]$ has $n^2$ geometric points over $\bar k$ (when $\operatorname{char}(k)\nmid n$), isomorphic to $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^2$.

questions.q: Two short Weierstrass curves over $\bar k$ are isomorphic iff:
choices: they have the same number of rational points over every finite field
the coefficients $(a,b)$ agree up to sign
questions.hint: $j(E)=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$ is a complete isomorphism invariant over $\bar k$.
questions.explain: $j(E)=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$ is a complete isomorphism invariant over $\bar k$.
With $b=0$, $j=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3)=1728$. This is the CM-by-$\mathbb{Z}[i]$ curve.

questions.hint: At $j=0$ the curve has an order-$6$ automorphism (via $\omega$); at $j=1728$ it has an order-$4$ automorphism (via $i$).
questions.explain: At $j=0$ the curve has an order-$6$ automorphism (via $\omega$); at $j=1728$ it has an order-$4$ automorphism (via $i$). Generic $j$ gives $\operatorname{Aut}(E)=\{\pm1\}$.

hard.q: Two curves $E: y^2=x^3+x$ and $E': y^2=x^3+4x$ have the same $j$-invariant $1728$. Over $\mathbb{Q}$, they are:
choices: Isomorphic only over $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$; over $\mathbb{Q}$ they are quadratic twists, hence generally non-isomorphic
Isomorphic iff both have the same rank
hard.hint: Find the scalar $u$ needed to match coefficients, then check if it lives in $\mathbb{Q}$.
hard.explain: Same $j$ over $\bar k$ means isomorphic over $\bar k$, but the twist parameter $u^4 a'=a$ requires $u^4=4$, so $u=\sqrt{2}\notin\mathbb{Q}$. They are $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$-twists of each other, not isomorphic over $\mathbb{Q}$.
$4a^3=0$, so $j=1728\cdot 0/(0+27)=0$. This is the CM-by-$\mathbb{Z}[\omega]$ curve.

hard.q: The isomorphism $(a,b)\mapsto(u^4 a, u^6 b)$ for $u\in\bar k^\times$ induces a $\bar k^\times$-action of weight $(4,6)$. The $j$-invariant $j=1728\cdot 4a^3/(4a^3+27b^2)$ is invariant because:
choices: Numerator and denominator both scale by $u^{12}$, so the ratio is unchanged
The discriminant $\Delta$ scales by $u^6$, cancelling the numerator
$j$ is always an integer, hence constant under field automorphisms
The $j$-line $\mathbb{A}^1_j$ has trivial $\bar k^\times$-action by definition
hard.explain: Under $(a,b)\mapsto(u^4 a,u^6 b)$: numerator $4a^3\mapsto 4u^{12}a^3$ and denominator $4a^3+27b^2\mapsto u^{12}(4a^3+27b^2)$. The $u^{12}$ cancels, leaving $j$ unchanged.

questions.q: Over $\mathbb{C}$, every elliptic curve is analytically isomorphic to:
choices: a quotient $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ by a rank-$2$ lattice
questions.hint: The Weierstrass $\wp$-function embeds $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ as a cubic $y^2=4x^3-g_2 x-g_3$ in $\mathbb{CP}^2$.
questions.explain: The Weierstrass $\wp$-function embeds $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ as a cubic $y^2=4x^3-g_2 x-g_3$ in $\mathbb{CP}^2$.

questions.q: An elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{C}$ has complex multiplication when:
questions.hint: CM means $\operatorname{End}(E)$ is an order in an imaginary quadratic field $K$; generically $\operatorname{End}(E)=\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.explain: CM means $\operatorname{End}(E)$ is an order in an imaginary quadratic field $K$; generically $\operatorname{End}(E)=\mathbb{Z}$.

questions.q: For the CM lattice $\Lambda=\mathbb{Z}[i]$, the endomorphism 'multiplication by $i$' has degree (i.e. number of sheets as an isogeny) equal to $|i|^2=$?
questions.hint: $|i|^2=1$, so multiplication by $i$ is an automorphism (degree $1$), consistent with $j=1728$ having $\operatorname{Aut}(E)\supset\{\pm1,\pm i\}$.
questions.explain: $|i|^2=1$, so multiplication by $i$ is an automorphism (degree $1$), consistent with $j=1728$ having $\operatorname{Aut}(E)\supset\{\pm1,\pm i\}$.

hard.q: Two lattices $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda'$ give isomorphic elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ iff:
hard.explain: An analytic isomorphism $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda\to\mathbb{C}/\Lambda'$ must lift to a $\mathbb{C}$-linear map $z\mapsto\alpha z$, which maps $\Lambda$ onto $\Lambda'$. So isomorphism $\Leftrightarrow$ homothety $\Lambda'=\alpha\Lambda$.

hard.q: The endomorphism 'multiplication by $1+i$' on $\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{Z}[i]$ is an isogeny of degree $|1+i|^2=$?
hard.explain: $|1+i|^2=1^2+1^2=2$. The kernel is $\frac{1}{1+i}\mathbb{Z}[i]/\mathbb{Z}[i]\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, so the isogeny has degree $2$.

hard.q: Which of the following correctly describes the significance of $j((1+\sqrt{-163})/2)=-640320^3$?
choices: It shows the $j$-function is unbounded on the upper half-plane
It is a CM $j$-value lying in $\mathbb{Q}$ because the Hilbert class number of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-163})$ is $1$, making $j$ rational and explaining why $e^{\pi\sqrt{163}}$ is nearly an integer
It proves the Riemann hypothesis for the $L$-function of the CM curve
It gives the order of the automorphism group of the corresponding elliptic curve
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-163})$ has class number $1$, so the Hilbert class field equals $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-163})$ and its singular modulus $j$ must lie in $\mathbb{Q}$. Since $j=-640320^3$, we get $e^{\pi\sqrt{163}}=j+744+O(e^{-2\pi\sqrt{163}})\approx 640320^3+744$.

questions.hint: Rank is the exponent of the free part.
questions.explain: Rank is the exponent of the free part.

questions.q: The torsion subgroup in Mordell–Weil is:
questions.hint: Over $\mathbb{Q}$, torsion is finite (Mazur classifies possibilities).
questions.explain: Over $\mathbb{Q}$, torsion is finite (Mazur classifies possibilities).

hard.q: Which of the following is NOT on Mazur's list of possible torsion subgroups of $E(\mathbb{Q})$?
hard.explain: Mazur's theorem (1977) restricts $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$ to $\mathbb{Z}/n$ for $n\le 10$ or $n=12$, or $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2n$ for $n\le 4$. $\mathbb{Z}/11$ is not on the list; the allowed cyclic groups go up to $n=10$ and then jump to $12$.

hard.q: The BSD conjecture predicts $\operatorname{ord}_{s=1}L(E,s)=r$. For which ranks is this unconditionally known?
choices: All ranks, by work of Wiles
Ranks $0$ and $1$, by Kolyvagin and Gross–Zagier
Only rank $0$, by Euler system methods
No ranks; BSD is entirely open
hard.explain: Gross–Zagier (1986) links the derivative $L'(E,1)$ to the height of a Heegner point, and Kolyvagin's Euler system bounds the Selmer group. Together these prove BSD for analytic rank $\le 1$.

hard.q: In the proof of the weak Mordell–Weil theorem, which exact sequence is central?
choices: The long exact sequence in singular homology for the pair $(E,\mathcal O)$
The Kummer sequence $0\to E[n]\to E\xrightarrow{[n]} E\to 0$ of group schemes, giving $0\to E(K)/nE(K)\to H^1(G_K,E[n])\to\ldots$ in Galois cohomology
The short exact sequence $0\to\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{R}\to S^1\to 0$ in topology
The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $E$ decomposed into two affine patches
hard.hint: You need a sequence that turns $E(K)/nE(K)$ into something cohomological.
hard.explain: Applying Galois cohomology to $0\to E[n]\to E\xrightarrow{[n]}E\to 0$ gives the Kummer map $E(K)/nE(K)\hookrightarrow H^1(G_K,E[n])$. Since $E[n]$ is defined over a finite extension and the cocycles satisfy local conditions, the image (the Selmer group) is finite, proving $E(K)/nE(K)$ is finite.
